note_data.title: Number
keyPoints: **Natural numbers** are positive integers: 1, 2, 3, 4, ... (used for counting)
**Rational numbers** can be expressed as p/q where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0
**Irrational numbers** cannot be expressed as a fraction (e.g., √2, π, surds)
**Standard form** is written as a × 10ⁿ where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an integer
When **ordering** numbers, convert all to the same form (decimals or fractions) first
**Percentage change** = (new value - original value) ÷ original value × 100%
In **ratio and proportion**, if a:b = c:d, then ad = bc (cross multiplication)
**Estimation** requires rounding numbers to 1 significant figure before calculating
**Surds** in simplest form have no perfect square factors under the square root sign
**Upper and lower bounds** depend on the degree of accuracy given in the question

definitions.definition: A natural number greater than 1 that has exactly two factors: 1 and itself
A number written in the form a × 10ⁿ where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an integer
An irrational number involving a square root that cannot be simplified to give a rational number
The meaningful digits in a number, counting from the first non-zero digit
A fraction expressed as parts per hundred, written with the symbol %

howToAnswer.instruction: Write the answer in the form requested (e.g., standard form, percentage, ratio)
Round all numbers to 1 significant figure, then calculate
Give the answer in its simplest form with no common factors
Show all working steps and give the exact numerical answer

modelAnswer.question: The population of Karachi is 1.47 × 10⁷. The population of Lahore is 1.13 × 10⁷. Calculate the difference and express your answer in standard form.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Final answer in correct standard form 3.4 × 10⁶

commonMistakes: Never write 0.34 × 10⁷ as final answer in standard form — always adjust to 3.4 × 10⁶
Students often forget that 1 ≤ a < 10 in standard form, writing answers like 14.7 × 10⁶
When converting fractions to percentages, students multiply by 10 instead of 100
In ratio problems, students add the ratios instead of using cross multiplication
Students round too early in multi-step calculations, losing accuracy marks
examinerTips: Standard form appears in almost every Paper 2, worth 2-4 marks typically
Always check your standard form answer: coefficient between 1 and 10
For estimation questions, show your rounded numbers clearly for method marks
Percentage questions often test reverse percentages — practice finding original values
formulas: Upper bound = measurement + (0.5 × unit of measurement)
Lower bound = measurement - (0.5 × unit of measurement)
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Number line showing position of different types of numbers (natural, integers, rational, irrational) and their relationships
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q2 (3 marks) — they asked students to use given random digits to select student numbers from a list, requiring understanding of systematic sampling methods
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q4 (5 marks) — students had to estimate values from a cumulative frequency graph and calculate the number of data points below a given threshold
This concept was examined in M/J 2024 P1 Q8 (4 marks) — candidates needed to find the probability that a sample mean exceeded a given value using normal distribution properties
This was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q6 (6 marks) — students had to solve trigonometric equations involving multiple solutions within a specified interval

workedExample.question: Find the probability that the mean number of characters in a random sample of 100 emails is more than 1300, given that the population follows N(1250, 480²). (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that we need the distribution of the sample mean. For sample size n=100 from N(1250, 480²), the sample mean follows N(1250, 480²/100) = N(1250, 48²)
Step 4: Using normal tables, Φ(1.04) ≈ 0.851, so P(Z > 1.042) = 1 - 0.851 = 0.149
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying sample mean distribution, 1 mark for correct standardization, 1 mark for using normal tables correctly, 1 mark for final answer

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused population standard deviation with sample mean standard deviation' — always divide σ by √n when dealing with sample means
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently rounded intermediate calculations too early' — keep at least 4 decimal places in working until the final answer
Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often forgot to subtract from 1 when finding upper tail probabilities' — remember P(Z > a) = 1 - P(Z ≤ a) from normal tables
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to sketch a normal distribution curve showing the mean (1250), the sample value (1300), and clearly shade the area representing P(X̄ > 1300) in the upper tail. The diagram should include the standardized z-value (1.042) on a second sketch showing the standard normal distribution N(0,1), with the shaded area clearly labeled as 0.149.
note_data.quickRecap: For normal distribution problems: identify if dealing with individual values or sample means (divide σ by √n for means). Always standardize using Z = (x-μ)/σ. Use normal tables to find probabilities, remembering upper tail = 1 - table value. Keep precision in calculations and clearly show the area you're finding on a sketch.
